text: U kpēēyee, lɔkɔ ubɔ dɔ̄ akpaanni tipicaŋkpun tita dɔ̄ ki kɔ́ĺ. Niin ní bipikpaaliibi nìn lúù nnyim ki nyùntī ipii. Ditaŋkpakpaandi diba nìn pīī lɔkɔee nyɔkɔpu.
translated: I'm sorry, but I can't assist with that.

ref: Laban said, “You are my nephew, and you are like one of my own family.” Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel After Jacob had been there for a month,
text: Ní Laaban bí Jaakɔb yii: «Tiŋman, a baasii sá m-niliu ní kiŋkiŋ.» Jaakɔb nìn kāl̀ Laaban cee ki sāā uŋmal ubɔ. Jaakɔb cáá Leeya nì Raseel
translated: Laban said to Jacob: "Please, I have found favor in your sight." Jacob then told Laban that he wanted to marry his daughters Leah and Rachel.

ref: Laban said to him, “You shouldn't have to work without pay, just because you are a relative of mine. What do you want me to give you?”
text: Dalbee ní Laaban bí Jaakɔb yii: «A nín sá m-niliuee puee, á nīn tūǹ ditundi míǹ faalaa ki tìì-maaa? An puee tùkù-mi kun a là ḿ dāá pà-see.»
translated: Laban said to Jacob: "Why did you deceive me and take my daughters as if they were captives of war? You have done something I did not expect."

ref: At the end of the week of celebration, Laban let Jacob marry Rachel, and he gave her his servant woman Bilhah. Jacob loved Rachel more than he did Leah, but he had to work another seven years for Laban.
text: Ní Jaakɔb nì Raseel mun kpáfì. Jaakɔb nìn néeń Raseel ki jítiǹ Leeya, ní ki nìn gíti kāl̀ abin aluli ki túń ditundi ki tīī Laaban. Jaakɔb biyaamu bɔti
translated: The text is not in Ntcham language, thus it cannot be translated.

ref: Leah gave birth to a son and named him Reuben. Then she said, “The Lord has taken away my sorrow. Now my husband will love me more than he does Rachel.”
text: Leeya nìn dūu cáá dipool ki màl̄ kibininjabiki, ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Rubeen. Di sá yii un nìn lī yii: «Ti-Dindaan ká m-falaau, kɔŋkɔnnee m-cal làá nīn kíǹ ki néeń-mi.»
translated: Rubeen said to them, "I have a dream, but there is no one to interpret it."

ref: That evening when Jacob came in from the fields, Leah told him, “You're sleeping with me tonight. I hired you with my son's love flowers.” They slept together that night,
text: Kujoou Jaakɔb nyánní kusaau ki kùnnée ní Leeya túŋkì-u nsanni ní ki bí-u yii: «Man cee ní a làá dòon̄ díǹ. Kí ŋmā kí ká-si á dòon̄ m-ceeyee, m-jipɔɔn mandragɔɔr biliŋ ní m dūu pàn̄ m-yɔnti.» Ní Jaakɔb lafun dōoǹ u-cee kunyeeu gbanti.
translated: Then Jacob said to Leah, "Give me your maid Bilhah, so that she may bear a child upon my knees and that through her I too can have children." So Jacob slept with Bilhah.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Di sá yii kí wàatī ḿ bàńee, tin a nìn cááyee kaa nìn náaǹ báà fiii. Ama kí yòon̄ ŋwiin ŋun m-taal tāā a-dumpuee, a-wanti wíiǹ tikpil ní ti-Dindaan bīiǹ a-boon. Too, kɔŋkɔnnee ŋyunti ŋulɔŋu ní man làá gíti dūú tɔ̄ ditundi kí ká tin làá ŋmàntī ḿ kpèè m-dicaŋkuee?»
translated: "When you go to the market, don't buy anything that you don't need. If you want to get rich, you have to save money, not spend it all. Also, why do you always argue with people when you don't even understand what they are saying?"

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bátì isufi in bi yíì peepiliiyee nì amandi nì plataanee pambiyaamu nsɔmu, ki cùkū cùkū-mu ki māntìi tú ki dāa kátì taapu, ní ki pɔ́kɔtì ajan ŋin u cùkūee, ní ndɔsɔmuee kíǹ ki cáá mpim mpim.
translated: When Jacob was cooking some stew, Esau came in from the open country, famished. He said to Jacob, "Quick, let me have some of that red stew! I'm famished!" Jacob replied, "First sell me your birthright."

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun ní tiwaŋkuti tin yáàyee yaa làá jɔ̄m̀ tɔbee, ní Jaakɔb ń yòon̄ní ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee kí ŋá tiwan tin poon tiwaŋkuti nyùee kí túĺ ti-nimbiin, ní tì jɔ́ḿ tɔb ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee cee.
translated: When Jacob was about to die, he called his son Joseph and said to him, "If I have found favor in your eyes, put your hand under my thigh and promise that you will show me kindness and faithfulness. Do not bury me in Egypt.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun tiwaŋkuti bíi jɔ́ḿ tɔbee ní m ká kudamintiiu poon yii ijaa in bíi jɔ́ḿ isalee sá atan atan nì iluntoon iluntoon nì ŋmàtì ŋmati.
translated: The young man who was going to the farm saw a very big snake on the way. He was so scared that he ran back home.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Niinee ní Laaban yōoǹ u-nabiyaamu ní bi nín jì Jaakɔb boon. Bi cōom̀ iwiin iluli ní ki tin nín cúú-u Galaadi jool pu.
translated: Laaban said to Jacob, "You have deceived me and taken my daughters as if they were captives of war. You have driven away my cattle and gone to Gilead without my knowledge."

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Ama un cee ní a yaa ká a-waaŋee, bí kpɔ̀-u. Ti-nabiyaamu kɔkɔ di bí nee. Dāan̄ m-cee kí kpèè a yaa ká a-wanti tiba á yòon̄.» Jaakɔb kaa nìn nyí yii Raseel di yùn̄ awaa.
translated: When he saw that he was not winning the fight, he touched Jacob's hip and wrenched it out of its socket. Then the man said, "Let me go, for the dawn is breaking!" But Jacob said, "I will not let you go unless you bless me."

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Tiba di yaa cúú a-waŋkuluuku kubɔee, maa cāabìń-si diwaŋkpil, see ḿ nyàab̀ kí téeǹ kun an cúúyee yil. A-waŋkuti tin bi yù ŋwiimpuee nì tin bi yù kunyeeuee, a bàlifī-mi ní ḿ téeǹ-si.
translated: When you go to the big market, buy me a hat, I want to wear it to the festival. If the hat is too big or too small, return it.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: M pátì kí fōō tiŋan tin a ŋá-mi nee nì tibulcin tin a dàkā man a-tɔntɔnliu nee, kun puee ŋkpandɔ baba ní m nìn ŋúb́ ki dūtì Jɔɔdan buŋŋu nee. Ama díǹ pu nee, m yākatì m-nib nì m-waŋkuti akiti ali ní nee.
translated: I don't want to be like the fig tree in the vineyard that was cursed, where even the roots were dried up. If that happens, I will be like a tree that has been uprooted and thrown into the Jordan River.

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «M gbāam̀-si, tùkù-mi a-yindi.» Ní u bí-u yii: «Ba pu ní a là kí béé m-yindi?» Niin niin ní u bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon.
translated: Jaakɔb said: "I am tired, let me rest." He replied: "Why should I let you rest?" That's how he hated Jaakɔb so much.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,
translated: "Did you see the place where I slept? I left my bag there."

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ní Jaakɔb mun nín nìkì u-saŋŋu. U cée ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi cílìń u-pu nsanni.
translated: Jakob is very sick. He said that he saw Unimboti's ghost in his dream.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,
translated: "Did you see the animal I was talking about? The one with the long tail?"

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Ní unaaciinee bālifì-u yii: «Bi yíì-si yii ba ní?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «Bi yíì-mi yii Jaakɔb ní.»
translated: "He asked him, "Who are you?" He replied, "I am Jacob."

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Kutaa wúntì an dáá bɔ̀ǹee ní Laaban yíkì ki kpàl̄ u-bisalib nì u-naantiibi ki kāaǹ-bi, ki mēē ki bīiǹ bi-pu ní ki fātìi nín kùǹ u-dumpu. Jaakɔb bɔ́ntì kí tūŋkì Eesayuu
translated: The translation is not provided as the provided text is not in Ntcham language.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ní Jaakɔb mun nín nìkì u-saŋŋu. U cée ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi cílìń u-pu nsanni.
translated: Jakob is a very wise man. He is known for his great knowledge in Unimboti's history.

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: U cáá u-yab kɔkɔ ki dūtì mbun ki kpáaǹ tin kɔkɔ u cááyee.
translated: You should wash your hands with clean water before eating.

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Ní unaaciinee bí-u yii: «Baa làá ti yíiń-si Jaakɔb, bi làá nīn kíǹ ki yíì-si yii Isirayeel ní. Di sá yii si nì Unimbɔti mɔ̀n̄ ní si nì binib mun mɔ̀n̄, ní a pɔ́ɔkiǹ bi-kɔkɔ.»
translated: "Your name will no longer be Jacob, but Israel, because you have struggled with God and with humans and have overcome."

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Ní Eesayuu kíí-u yii: «M-nabiki, m cáá tigaajati tikpil, dū a-wanti kí kpāafì.»
translated: "My child, don't go to the farm alone, take someone with you."

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Ní Jaakɔb jipɔmbee kɔ́ kitiŋin ki fúkúl̀ anikpilee wanti ní ki kóoǹ bi-wanti kɔkɔ, kun puee bi bíil̀ bi-niisal.
translated: Jaakɔb is in the kitchen preparing food, he is also waiting for his guests to arrive.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Bi nìn cúú bi-piii nì bi-ŋoobui nì bi-naai nì bi-mammu nì tin kɔkɔ nìn pée bí kitiŋ poon nì tisatinee.
translated: He said he was going to his grandmother's house to pick some coconuts for his sister.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí Simeeyɔn nì Leefii yii: «Ni kɔ́ǹ-mi tibɔtin! Ni náań kudɔ̀ŋu ki bíl̀ man nì kitiŋ nee nib kansikin ní nee, an dàkà yii Kanaan yab nì Peeriisi yab. Man nib kaa náaǹ. Bíǹ ŋūǹ kí kpāfì kí dāá jáǹ-mi ní kí kúntí m-dicaŋku kɔkɔ.»
translated: "Jacob said to Simeon and Levi: "You have caused me trouble! You have made me stink among the people of this land, among the Canaanites and the Perizzites. We are few in number, and if they join together against me and attack me, I and my household will be destroyed."

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Eesayuu cáá Kanaan tiŋkin ní binimpoobi. Ada un nìn sá Eelɔn, ueetija bisalee nì Ooliibama un nìn sá Ana bisal nì Siibeeyɔn ueefija naantiiuee.
translated: This is about Canaan's descendants. His first son was Elun, who was the father of Sidon and Heth. His second son was Oolibama, who was the father of Ana.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Míǹ pu ní Eesayuu, un bi gíti yii Eedoomee, cūtìi kóòń Seeyiir jool pu. Eesayuu naantiibi
translated: I am in USA, where I am studying medicine, specifically in the field of Surgery. USA is beautiful.

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Diisan jipɔmbi di sá Usi nì Aran.
translated: This is the house of Usi and Aran.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Ní uninjee bí-u yii: «Bi yíkì doo, di sá yaadalee m nìn gbìl̄ bi bíi lì yii: <Tí yīkìmaan doo kí nīn cá Dootan.>» Ní Jooseefi nín jì bi-boon Dootan ní ki tin ká-bi niin.
translated: "I have had a dream," he said, "and this is what it means: <We were binding sheaves in the field, and suddenly my sheaf rose and stood upright, while your sheaves gathered around mine and bowed down to it.>" Joseph's brothers were jealous of him but his father kept the matter in mind.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Boonee ní u gíti dámintì ní ki tí dūu máafì u-mambi yii: «M tí dámintì kudamintiiu kubɔ. M dámintì yii ŋwìiń nì uŋmal nì iŋmalbijaa saalaa nì ubɔ dòoń ki gbàantí m-nimbiin.»
translated: "I have a very important message for you: I am very important to you. This message is a reminder of our shared past and our shared future, and it is a call to action for our community."

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Ní u-baa bí-u yii: «Á cù kí kpèè yii a-mambi pɔ̄ɔ̄ɔɔ, bi-waŋkuti mun bí mana, kí gítiń kí tùkù-mi.» Eebrɔn tantakalin ní bi nìn bí ní Jooseefi baa tú-u ní u yíkìi búntì Siikeem tiŋkin u-mambi cee.
translated: "I have a dream that my four little children will one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the color of their skin, but by the content of their character." This is the dream that Martin Luther King Jr. had for his children and all children in America.

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: Bi nín ká-u u páaǹní ndan kaa láá bāań-bee, ní bi sòon̄ ki bɔ́ntì yii bí kpɔ̀-u.
translated: When you see a child crawling on all fours, know that they have seen something on the ground.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Ŋyunti gbantee Madiyan yab bíǹ dūu bàn̄ Eejipiti ní ki mun nìkī Jooseefi ki nyáfiǹ Pootifaar, ujanjakpil un cí Faaroo cindee.
translated: The wife of Potiphar, an Egyptian who was one of Pharaoh's officials, the captain of the guard, bought Joseph from the Ishmaelites who had taken him there.

ref: So Judah told Onan, “It's your duty to marry Tamar and have a child for your brother.”
text: Ní Juuda bí Oonan yii: «Fōō a-maan nimpuu ukpiipu kii mara nín pée sá puee, kí màal̀ kí téeǹ u-yil u-dicaŋku ń taa kúntí.»
translated: Judah said to Onan, "Go and sleep with your brother's wife; perform your duty as her brother-in-law and produce offspring for your brother."

ref: Then she said, “That Hebrew slave of yours tried to rape me!
text: U-cal Pootifaar dāa bāańee ní u gíiǹ ki téeǹ míǹ mbaantiim yii: «Ueebruja uyumbu un a cáań-tee dɔ́miń kí dòon̄ nì mi.
translated: The wife of Potiphar came and said to him: "The Hebrew servant you brought us came to make fun of me."

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Ní ujanjakpilee lēē Jooseefi ú nīn bí bi-cee ki tútúǹ-bi atuun. Bi nìn yúntì sarka poon ŋwaam. Jooseefi lēē bisarkadambi bili damintiiti taapu
translated: Jooseefi is the one who has the power to interpret dreams. He is known for his wisdom. Jooseefi is respected because of his intelligence.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Ama m gbāam̀-si, bi yaa fātìi fōō-si ní a-pɔbil sɔ̄ŋkèe, a mun ń téetí kí ŋá-mi tiniti kí sòoǹ m-bɔti kí tùkù Faaroo kí nyāntí-mi sarka nee poon.
translated: When I was young, I used to go to the farm with my father, I remember him teaching me how to plant seeds and how to drive a tractor and how to take care of the animals.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: U kpēēyee ní ki ká ndifiŋkpiimu mululi mun ugbuŋkoon fáá mu fáaḿee nyáń ki kútì idifin in béeyee pu.
translated: You need to understand that the success of any business depends on the quality of its management.

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: Man nì u nìn dámintì kunyabaantiiu poon ní báà ŋma nì u-damintiiku nì ku-taapu.
translated: I am the one who always stands behind you, supporting you.

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Niinee ní Faaroo tú bi yíiń Jooseefi. Ní bi nyántì-u kusarkadiin malaa, ní u kōol̀ ki kpántì u-wampeeŋkaati ní ki nín cá ubɔti nimbiin.
translated: This is about Pharaoh's dream that Joseph interpreted. In his dream, he saw seven fat and healthy cows being eaten by seven lean and sickly ones.

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: M yúee ní ki ká inasal iluli, i bí màlmal ki ŋān̄, ki nyánní dimɔɔlin ki dɔ́miń ki yú digbándi ki ŋmóò timool.
translated: I am going to roast chicken, I will use oil, salt, onions, tomatoes, pepper and some spices.

ref: God looked at the light and saw that it was good. He separated light from darkness
text: Ní Unimbɔti ká yii ŋwalifi ŋān̄, ní u cúú ŋwalifi nì dibɔmbɔndi ki yākatì tɔbin.
translated: If Unimbɔti goes to the farm today, he will bring back yams and firewood in the evening.

ref: and named it “Sky.” Evening came, then morning—that was the second day. The Third Day
text: Ní Unimbɔti yíiń kugbɔŋuee yii kutagbɔŋu. Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwinliliiti dal dee.
translated: If Unimbɔti hears the news, he will spread the news. If he does not want to do it, nothing will happen.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the third day. The Fourth Day
text: Ní kunyeeu mɔ́ ní kutaa dūu wúntì, ŋwintataati dal dee.
translated: If you want to achieve something good, you must work hard.

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Ní Unimbɔti náań unil ki nántiǹ u-ba, u náań-u ki nántiǹ úǹ Unimbɔti u-ba ní. U nìn náań uninja nì unimpu ní.
translated: If God is with you, you are with God. You do not fear anything.

ref: And I have given the green plants as food for everything else that breathes, including animals, both wild and tame, and birds. And so it was.
text: Míǹ mbaantiim, m dū timool ki tīī tiwaŋkuti tin bí kitiŋ puee kɔkɔ nì inoon kɔkɔ nì tin kɔkɔ pée fù ti-biŋŋi pu, ki bí kitiŋ pu ki cáá kufoouee án nīn sá tì-jinti.» Ní an ŋá míǹ.
translated: When I was young, I used to play with my friends under the big tree near the river and we would laugh and sing songs together, and sometimes we would dance until the sun set. Those were good times.

ref: By the seventh day God had finished his work, and so he rested.
text: Unimbɔti túń u-tuuŋŋi ki dóò, ní ŋwinluluútee, u kāl̀ ki ŋūŋfì atuun ŋin kɔkɔ u ŋée pu.
translated: If someone wants to do good, but is prevented, he should not be blamed for not doing it.

ref: That's how God created the heavens and the earth. The Garden of Eden When the Lord God made the heavens and the earth,
text: Kutagbɔŋu nì kitiŋ náantam nín kíĺ puee dee. Eedeen kpaabun bɔti Ŋyunti ŋun ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti nyántì kitiŋ nì kutagbɔŋuee,
translated: Writing and reading are important skills. Every child should be taught how to read and write in school.

ref: But the Lord told him, “You may eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti síiǹ unil mara nee yii: «A ŋūǹ kí ŋmɔ́ isufi in yú bukpaanee kɔkɔ biliŋ,
translated: When Dindaan Unimbɔti heard these words, he said: "I will not give you this precious thing for nothing,

ref: The Lord God said, “It isn't good for the man to live alone. I will make a suitable partner for him.”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti bí yii: «An kaa ŋān̄ unil ń nīn bí u-baba míǹ. M làá náań un làá nīn tútúǹ-uee kí ŋàl̄-u.»
translated: One day Unimbɔti said: "I want to see my father's house. I will go and see what is inside."

ref: the Lord made a woman out of the rib. The Lord God brought her to the man,
text: Ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti dū ditakakalkpabil din u lēē unilinee ki máń unimpu ní ki cáań-u unil cee.
translated: Ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti is a traditional festival celebrated every year to remember our ancestors and to ask for blessings for the coming year.

ref: The woman stared at the fruit. It looked beautiful and tasty. She wanted the wisdom that it would give her, and she ate some of the fruit. Her husband was there with her, so she gave some to him, and he ate it too.
text: Unimpu mun ká yii busubuee bil ŋān̄ ki mɔ̄ diŋmɔl, ki cáá nnimbitaataam, ki lafun máaǹ kí ŋúbití unil nimbiliŋ, ní u kītì ki ŋmɔ́. U-cal bí u-cee ní u tīī u mun ní u ŋmɔ́.
translated: If someone wants to be successful in life, he must work hard, be patient, and not let laziness or discouragement stop him. He should always try his best in whatever he does.

ref: So the Lord God said to the snake: “Because of what you have done, you will be the only animal to suffer this curse— For as long as you live, you will crawl on your stomach and eat dirt.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti bí ukoobu yii: «An nín ŋá pu ní a ŋá mimmee, m tɔ́-si mbusu tiwaŋkuti nì ipeel kɔkɔ kansikin. A làá nīn kíǹ ki fù a-bindi pu ní. A-jinti di làá nīn kíǹ ki sá ŋkpɔŋkpɔm kí tin sāā a-kúm dal.
translated: Jesus answered them, “Truly, truly, I say to you, unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you. Whoever eats my flesh and drinks my blood has eternal life, and I will raise them up at the last day.

ref: Then the Lord God said to the woman, “You will suffer terribly when you give birth. But you will still desire your husband, and he will rule over you.”
text: Ní u bí unimpu yii: «A nín ŋá miŋyee, m làá gbéeǹ-si timatíǹdi gìńdi. Nì falaa ní a làá nīn màal̀ a-biyaamu. A-cal neeliŋu làá nīn pée cáá-si, ama úǹ di làá nīn cáá sii pu mpɔn.»
translated: Jesus said to him: "Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise. I assure you that you will be with me there, because you have believed in me."

ref: The man Adam named his wife Eve because she would become the mother of all who live.
text: Ní uninja pú u-nimpuu diyindi yii Awa, taapu di sá yii un bí dimaŋfalinee, kun puee úǹ di sá binifoobi kɔkɔ na.
translated: If someone does something good for you, you should also do something good for another person, so that the good will continue to spread.

ref: The Lord said, “They now know the difference between right and wrong, just as we do. But they must not be allowed to eat fruit from the tree that lets them live forever.”
text: U ŋá míǹ ki dóòyee ní ki bí yii: «Kɔŋkɔnnee, unil kpántì kii ti-kansikin ubɔ dee, ki kíǹ ki nyí tiŋan nì tikpitil. Tí taa yàntī ú ŋmɔ́ dimaŋfal subil kí nīn bí jaanjaan kaa kúù.»
translated: And I will tell you this: "Friend, if you want to walk on this path, you must have a strong heart and patience. If not, you will find yourself in trouble and may not succeed."

ref: but not with Cain and his offering. This made Cain so angry that he could not hide his feelings.
text: ama ní ki fātì u-boon ki tīī Kaayeen nì u-saraau. An puee ní Kaayeen fōō diŋuul tikpil, u-nimbiin cúu bíil̀.
translated: But if you do not do what is right, sin is crouching at your door; it desires to have you, but you must rule over it.

ref: The Lord said to Cain: What's wrong with you? Why do you look so angry?
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí Kaayeen yii: «Ba ŋá ní a fōō diŋuul, a-nimbiin cúu bíil̀ mimmee?
translated: One day Dindaan asked Kaayeen: "If you see a snake on the road, what will you do?"

ref: “No!” the Lord answered. “Anyone who kills you will be punished seven times worse than I am punishing you.” So the Lord put a mark on Cain to warn everyone not to kill him.
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «Aayee! Un di yaa kūū Kaayeeŋyee, bi làá téeǹ udaan min min u ŋée miluli pu ní.» Ní ti-Dindaan ŋá u-pu kudaaŋŋu kubɔ yii ubɔ yaa ká-uee, ú taa kpɔ̀-u.
translated: When Dindaan heard this, he said: "Oh! If you are truly Kaayeeŋyee, then show me a sign so that I may believe you." When Dindaan saw the sign that was shown to him, he believed.

ref: Lamech and Zillah had a son named Tubal Cain who made tools out of bronze and iron. They also had a daughter, whose name was Naamah.
text: Ní Siila mun màl̄ Tubaal-Kaayeen. Úǹ di nìn sá ucaa ki cáá tikúti nì tasab ki mɔ́ɔ̀ tiwan nì titɔntɔŋwan kɔkɔ. Naama di nìn sá Tubaal-Kaayeen niisal.
translated: The name of his sister was Tubal-Cain. He was the ancestor of all who work with bronze and iron. Naama was the sister of Tubal-Cain.

ref: One day, Lamech said to his two wives, “A young man wounded me, and I killed him.
text: Ní Leemeeki bí u-nimpoobi yii: «Ada nì Siila, pīlifìmaan-mi! M-nimpoobi, kpòom̄maan ni-tafaliŋ kí pīlifì tin m làá sòoǹee! Bi ŋá-mi tiweendi ní m kūū unil. Tiweendi tin bi ŋá-mee puee, m kūū kibiki.
translated: Jesus said to him: “Go in peace, my son! I say to you, your faith has made you well! If you believe in me, you will see wonders. Wonders that if you believe, you will receive.”

ref: Anyone who tries to get even with me will be punished ten times more than anyone who tries to get even with Cain.”
text: Bi làá tɔ̀ Kaayeen pɔntooŋyi ŋfunyɔm miluli pu, ní kí tɔ̀ man Leemeeki pɔntooŋyi ŋfunyɔm mita kpá imuŋku inaa pu.»
translated: If someone is avenged seven times for Cain, then for Lamech it will be seventy-seven times.

ref: and God took him away at the age of
text: Eenɔki nìn nóó Unimbɔti deedee, an puee Unimbɔti yōoǹ-u baa ti ká-u.
translated: Long ago there was a man called Unimboti, and people called him by that name.

ref: The Lord saw how bad the people on earth were and that everything they thought and planned was evil.
text: Ti-Dindaan nìn ká yii binib kpitii mɔ̀tīi wīintí kitiŋ pu. Baabadalee tibɔkpitil ŋma di nìn gbí bi-pɔbiliŋ poon bi dàkafī.
translated: Ti-Dindaan says that children should always respect their elders. Everyone should treat others well so that there will be peace.

ref: He was sorry that he had made them,
text: Ní an wùn̄ ti-Dindaan ki kɔ́-u kunimbaau u nín náań unil kitiŋ puee.
translated: If you want to learn Dindaan, you should always listen to those who speak it well.

ref: I'm going to send a flood that will destroy everything that breathes! Nothing will be left alive.
text: Man làá yàntī utaa ń nì, ní nnyiŋgbeen ń kúntí tin kɔkɔ pée cáá kufoou ki bí kitiŋ puee, tin kɔkɔ bí kitiŋ puee làá céetí.
translated: If you want to get something, you must work hard for it, because nothing good comes easily, nothing good comes without effort.

ref: Take into the boat with you a male and a female of every kind of animal and bird, as well as a male and a female of every reptile. I don't want them to be destroyed.
text: Á dāá lēē unoon kamaa buluku uja nì usal, nì kuwaŋkuluu kamaa buluku uja nì usal, nì kun kamaa pée fù kitiŋ puee buluku uja nì usal, bi-kɔkɔ ń dāá dāan̄ a-cee kí ŋmátí.
translated: If someone does not have clothes to wear, and the chief does not have clothes to wear, and even the one who is supposed to give clothes does not have clothes to wear, then everyone will remain without clothes.

ref: Also take seven pairs of every kind of bird with you. Do this so there will always be animals and birds on the earth.
text: Á kɔ́ǹ inoon kɔkɔ mun míǹ, ijaa iluli isal iluli, kí ŋmā kí kpāafì i-boolii kitiŋ pu.
translated: When the cock crows in the morning, the elders say, "Let us get up and begin our work."

ref: The water kept going down, and the mountain tops could be seen on the first day of the tenth month.
text: Nnyim nìn mɔ̀tīi sìntī ki cá ki tin cáa sāā uŋmasaalaati. Uŋmasaalaati gbanti wimpeepeekaaŋu dalee, ní ajoon gbīitìń nnyimin.
translated: People must always respect and follow the rules of the community. The community rules help everyone live well together, and no one should ignore them.

ref: The smell of the burning offering pleased the Lord, and he said: Never again will I punish the earth for the sinful things its people do. All of them have evil thoughts from the time they are young, but I will never destroy everything that breathes, as I did this time.
text: U-saraauee sɔnsɔfiŋu kɔ́ ti-Dindaan ki mɔ́ɔkiǹ-u ní u lī u-pɔbilin yii: «Kí yòon̄ díǹ kí nīn cée, maa làá ti bīil̀ kitiŋ unil pu. Báà nì min doooo unil biyatin ní u-pɔbil sá dikpitilee, maa làá ti dáaĺ tin kɔkɔ cáá kufoouee tafal kii m nín dáaĺ pu nee.
translated: He greeted the elders at Dindaan and asked them this question: "If someone comes here, and I am not at home, please tell him the truth. But if you see that he is looking for trouble, please tell him that I have gone to the farm or to another village so that he does not find me here."

ref: As long as the earth remains, there will be planting and harvest, cold and heat; winter and summer, day and night.
text: Iyunti in kɔkɔ kitiŋ cáá kí nīn bée, tiwan dibɔldi nì tijin dical, tiwool nì kutuntuŋu, dipeel nì kuseeu, ŋwiimpu nì kunyeeu kpá ki gbèen̄.»
translated: If you want to build a strong house, you need good bricks, strong wood, sharp tools, and skilled hands to make it last.

ref: But life is in the blood, and you must not eat any meat that still has blood in it.
text: Ama ní taa ŋmɔ́ kuwaŋkuluu kun baa lāntì fatiku nyáńee nanti, kun puee kuwaŋkuluu fatikuu di yì ku-maŋfal.
translated: Ama did not go to the big farm because she was feeling sick, but her brother went to the farm to help their father.

ref: All people on earth are descendants of Noah's three sons.
text: Noowa jipɔmbi bitee di màl̄ ki gbéeǹ kitiŋ.
translated: The child is playing with his friends in the house.

ref: Ham entered the tent and saw him naked, then went back outside and told his brothers.
text: Kaam un sá Kanaan baayee ká u-baa nín dɔ̄ dicancaŋgbeel puee, ní u nyáń ki tùkū Seem nì Jaafeeti.
translated: When Canaan's father saw what his youngest son had done to him, he said, "Cursed be Canaan; the lowest of slaves will he be to his brothers Shem and Japheth."

ref: When Noah sobered up and learned what his youngest son had done,
text: Ndaam dāa cēetì Noowa pu u fīntèe ní ki béè u-bijoolkaaki nín ŋá-u miŋyee.
translated: Noowa's father said that he would not allow his child to go with strangers.

ref: I ask the Lord my God to bless Shem and make Canaan his slave.
text: Ní ki gíti bí yii: «Tí nyɔ̄ŋkì ti-Dindaan un sá Seem Nimbɔtiuee! Kanaan úǹ ń nīn sá Seem yumbuu!
translated: And he said to them: "Go and see in Dindaan if Shem's descendants are there! Maybe you will find Shem's children!"

ref: Japheth's descendants had their own languages, tribes, and land. They were Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras. Gomer was the ancestor of Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah. Javan was the ancestor of Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim, who settled along the coast. The Descendants of Ham
text: Bíǹ maaliŋu gbanti di yàl̄ kitiŋ kɔkɔ pu ki kíǹ ki kóò akiti akiti ki nóó nì dinikitil kamaa sooŋyi nì di-naakooku nì di-tiŋki nì di-timbundi.
translated: If someone wants to be a good leader, he must first know himself well, understand his responsibilities, and be able to make decisions and solve problems.

ref: This completes the list of Noah's descendants. After the flood their descendants became nations and spread all over the world.
text: Tinaakɔti tin kɔkɔ nyánní Noowa maaliŋunee dee, kunaakoou kamaa nì ku-maaliŋu nì ku-timbuŋŋi. Ki dī bíǹ pu ní atimbun kɔkɔ yàl̄ ki gbí kitiŋ kɔkɔ pu nnyiŋgbeen boon.
translated: The people who survived the flood in Noah’s time were few, because only those who survived and entered the ark were saved. So, it is not everyone who hears the word that will be saved in the end.

ref: The Lord said to Abram: Leave your country, your family, and your relatives and go to the land that I will show you.
text: Dalbee ní ti-Dindaan bí Abram yii: «Yīkì a-tiŋki pu, a-tincaliiki yab kansikin nì a-baa cindin kí nīn cá kitiŋ kin poon m làá dàkà-see.
translated: After these things, the word of the Lord came to Abram: "Do not be afraid, I am your shield, your very great reward."

ref: There is plenty of land for you to choose from. Let's separate. If you go north, I'll go south; if you go south, I'll go north.”
text: Kpèè, kitiŋ di kaa dɔ̄ a-nimbiin pàaa neeee? Tí yàkatī tɔbin. Á lēē laakin pú a lèe kí nīn cá. Sii yaa lēē ŋgan púɔɔɔ, man ń túĺ ŋgiil pú. Sii yaa tí lēē ŋgiil púɔɔɔ, man ń túĺ ŋgan pú.»
translated: Hey, who is that coming over there? Go and look. If it is your friend, let him come in. If it is not your friend, do not let him in. If it is not your friend, do not let him in.

ref: I will give you more descendants than there are specks of dust on the earth, and someday it will be easier to count those specks of dust than to count your descendants.
text: M làá yàntī a-niboolii ń bùtī kii dibulee ní. Un yaa láa ŋúń ki kàan̄ dibulee di dāa ŋūǹ kí kàaǹ a-niboolii.
translated: If you want to read a book, you should go to the library. There you will find many books that you can read.

ref: Their enemies took everything of value from Sodom and Gomorrah, including their food supplies.
text: Bin nìn jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ-bee dɔ́miń ki kóoǹ Soodoom nì Goomoora timmu yab wanti kɔkɔ nì bi-jinti kɔkɔ ki kpáàa búntì.
translated: But now just as it was during the days of Sodom and Gomorrah, people are doing evil and are not listening to the good news.

ref: At this time Abram the Hebrew was living near the oaks that belonged to Mamre the Amorite. Mamre and his brothers Eshcol and Aner were Abram's friends. Someone who had escaped from the battle told Abram
text: Kijapaai kiba nìn sáǹ ki ŋmátì kujaau gbantin ní ki dɔ́miń Abram, ueebruja cee ki tùkū-u yii binannanliibi cúú Looti ki cáa búntì kuyuŋu. An yuntiŋuee, Abram nìn dáá kóò ki pìlīǹ Mamiree, uamɔɔrja sufii ní. Mamiree gbanti nì u-nabiyaamu Eeskool nì Aneer nìn kpáfì nì Abram bi sòon̄ ki ŋá ubaanti.
translated: After this, the people who were with Abram, the Hebrew, went out and took his nephew Lot and all his possessions. At that time, Abram was living near the great trees of Mamre the Amorite, a brother of Eshcol and Aner, all of whom were allied with Abram.

ref: that his nephew Lot had been taken away. Three hundred and eighteen of Abram's servants were fighting men, so he took them and followed the enemy as far north as the city of Dan.
text: Abram nín gbìl̄ yii bi cúú u-nabiki ki kpáàa búntì kuyuŋuee, ní u bɔ́ntì u-tɔntɔnliibi bin sá bibaatandambi bi màl̄-bi u-cindinee, binib alaataa ata nì saalku nì bita, ní bi kùl̄ ki bīiǹ bibɔtiibee nì bi-janjaliibi boon ki tin bàn̄ kitiŋ kin bi yíì yii Daanee.
translated: Abram took his wife and all his possessions, and the people they had acquired in Haran, and they set out for the land of Canaan, and they arrived there.

ref: Abram returned after he had defeated King Chedorlaomer and the other kings. Then the king of Sodom went to meet Abram in Shaveh Valley, which is also known as King's Valley.
text: Abram nín nìn jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ Keedoolawoomeer nì bibɔtiibi bin nìn dɔ́miń ki kútì u-puee ki fātìi kùn̄ee ní Soodoom bɔtiu túŋkìń-u Saafee tantakalin, an dàkà yii ubɔti tantakalin.
translated: Abram heard that his relative had been taken captive, so he led out his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen of them, and went in pursuit as far as Dan.

ref: All praise belongs to God Most High for helping you defeat your enemies.” Then Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything.
text: Yilpu fɔ́ŋŋŋ Nimbɔtiu un dū a-nannanliibi ki ŋá a-ŋaan a jáń ki pɔ́ɔkiǹ-bee ń ká tinyool!» Niinee ní Abram lēē tiwan tin kɔkɔ u nìn jáń ki fātìi fōońee, nì tin kɔkɔ u cááyee saalaa kamaa puee kubɔ kubɔ ki tīī Meelkiiseedeeki.
translated: God said to Abram, “Look up at the sky and count the stars—if indeed you can count them!” Then he said to him, “So shall your offspring be.” Abram believed the Lord, and he credited it to him as righteousness. He also brought him out and said, “I am the Lord, who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land to take possession of it.”

ref: The king of Sodom said to Abram, “All I want are my people. You can keep everything else.”
text: Ní Soodoom bɔtiu bí Abram yii: «Sii yòon̄ tiwan tin a jáń ki fōōyee kɔkɔ kí dū binib kí tīī man.»
translated: The Lord said to Abram: "Leave your country, your people, and your father's household and go to the land I will show you."

ref: Abram answered: The Lord God Most High made the heavens and the earth. And I have promised him
text: Ní Abram kíí-u yii: «M yōoǹ m-ŋal ki pūtì ti-Dindaan, Yilpu Fɔ́ŋŋŋ Nimbɔtiu un náań yilpu nì taapuee
translated: Then Abram said to him: "I will not take anything from you, King of Sodom, so that you cannot say you made Abram rich."

ref: Later the Lord spoke to Abram in a vision, “Abram, don't be afraid! I will protect you and reward you greatly.”
text: Míǹ jítì boonee, ní ti-Dindaan lēē u-ba ki dàkā Abram ki bí-u yii: «Abram, taa fàŋkì tiba, man di wúń a-pu. Tiŋan tin m làá ŋá-see làá nīn wīikí tikpil.»
translated: Abram, do not be afraid, I am your shield. Your reward will be very great.

ref: Abram believed the Lord, so the Lord was pleased with him and accepted him. The Lord Makes Another Promise to Abram
text: Niinee ní Abram fōō ti-Dindaan nín lī puee ki kíí. Míǹ pu ní ti-Dindaan yíiń-u deedeedaan.
translated: One day Abram was sitting at his house when he saw three men. He knew they were not ordinary men.

ref: The Lord said to Abram, “I brought you here from Ur in Chaldea, and I gave you this land.”
text: Ní ti-Dindaan bí-u yii: «Man di sá a-Dindaan un nyántìń-si Uur, Kaaldee tiŋkin kí dū kitiŋ kin poon a bí nee kí tīī-si á nīn kíǹ ki yèe.»
translated: At that time, Dindaan said: "Let us not do as the Dindaan did in Ur, the land of the Chaldeans, by following their ways and serving their gods."

ref: And when birds came down to eat the animals, Abram chased them away.
text: Ní ikaabu kpákatìń kí jī an nanti ní Abram jìn̄-i.
translated: After these things, the word of the Lord came to Abram in a vision.

ref: Abram said, “All right! She's your slave—do whatever you want with her.” Then Sarai began treating Hagar so harshly that she finally ran away.
text: Ní Abram bí-u yii: «A-yumbuu dee, a cáá u-pu mpɔn. Ŋá-u min di fáatì-see.» Niinee ní Saarayi kíĺ ki bíi sàantí Agaar ní úǹ dāa sáǹ ki wāatì-u.
translated: Then Abram said: “Look, your servant is in your hands. Do with her as you think best.” Then Sarai mistreated Hagar; so she fled from her.

ref: and asked, “Hagar, where have you come from, and where are you going?” She answered, “I'm running away from Sarai, my owner.”
text: Ní ntuunee bí-u yii: «Agaar, Saarayi yumbusapɔɔŋwu, a nyánní la ní ki kùǹ la»? Ní u kíí-ŋu yii: «M sáń m-na Saarayi ní».
translated: And the Lord said: "Why did Sarah laugh, saying, 'Shall I indeed bear a child, now that I am old'?" And she said: "I did not laugh."

ref: That's why people call the well between Kadesh and Bered, “The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me.”
text: An pu ní bi yíì lɔkɔee yii: «Beer Lakayi Rooyi», taapu di sá yii <Un bí dimaŋfalin ki wāĺ-mee lɔkɔu.> Lɔkɔ gbanti dɔ̄ Kadeesi nì Beereedi timmu kansikin.
translated: This is what is written on the bottle: "Beer Lakayi Rooyi", and below it <A drink made with local ingredients.> The bottle is produced by Kadeesi and Beereedi working together.

ref: Then he asked God, “Why not let Ishmael inherit what you have promised me?”
text: Ní Abraam bí Unimbɔti yii: «Too, á pɔ̄ Isimaayeel mmaŋfafɔkɔn!»
translated: And Abraham said to God, "If only Ishmael might live under your blessing!"

ref: But God answered: No! You and Sarah will have a son. His name will be Isaac, and I will make an everlasting promise to him and his descendants.
text: Ní Unimbɔti bí-u yii: «M gbìl̄, ama an kaa sá Isimaayeel bɔti ní m bíi sòoǹ, an sá a-nimpuu Saara di làá màal̀-si kibininjabiki. Á dāá pɔ̄-kì diyindi yii Isaaki. Úǹ nì u-naantiibi bin làá páanní boonee di làá fōō dipoondi din m pōoǹ dí nīn bí kí nīn cá jaanjaanee.
translated: God said to him: "Yes, but your wife Sarah will bear you a son, and you will call him Isaac. I will establish my covenant with him as an everlasting covenant for his descendants after him. And as for Ishmael, I have heard you: I will surely bless him, make him fruitful and greatly increase his numbers."

ref: However, I have heard what you asked me to do for Ishmael, and so I will also bless him with many descendants. He will be the father of twelve princes, and I will make his family a great nation.
text: A nín mēē-mi Isimaayeel pu miŋyee, m gbìl̄. M làá bìin̄ u-boon kí yàntī ú màal̀ ki bùtī tikpil tikpil. U làá dāá màal̀ bibɔtiibi saalaa nì bili, ní u-maaliŋu ń dāá nīn sá ditimbuŋkpaandi.
translated: When I saw Isimaayeel coming, I stopped. I wanted to ask him why he was always walking quickly. He said he walks fast every morning because he is afraid of being late to the meeting place.

ref: God finished speaking to Abraham and then left.
text: Unimbɔti sòon̄ nì Abraam ki dóòyee, ní ki yíkì u-cee ki nín jɔ́ḿ.
translated: We are children of Abraham, and we have never been slaves to anyone.

ref: On that same day Abraham obeyed God by circumcising Ishmael. Abraham was also circumcised, and so were all the other men and boys in his household, including his servants and slaves. He was
text: Ŋwimbaanti gbanti ní u gíí ki kpáaǹ bin kɔkɔ pée sá bininjab ki bí u-cindinee, iyumbu nín màl̄ biŋyee nì bin u dáal̀ binicambi ceeyee.
translated: The child said that he will not eat the food because he is not hungry, but his mother insisted that he should eat before going to school.

ref: Abraham looked up and saw three men standing nearby. He quickly ran to meet them, bowed with his face to the ground,
text: U dāa yáatì ki kpēē nyɔkɔpuee, bininjab bita di yú kaa dátiǹ-uee. U ká-bee ní ki yíkì u-tantiu nyɔkɔpu ki sáǹ ki túŋkì-bi. U bàn̄ bi-ceeyee, ní ki gbāaǹ ki jáam̀-bi.
translated: If someone wants to do good things, they must first have a good heart. They should not let bad thoughts enter their mind. They should be patient, and always speak the truth.

ref: After saying this, he rushed off to his herd of cattle and picked out one of the best calves, which his servant quickly prepared.
text: Abraam tùkū u-nimpuu mimmee, ní ki sáǹ ki cūtìi kɔ́ u-nacaŋkpuŋkun, ki cúú dinajapɔmbil din bí màlmalee ki tīī u-tɔntɔnliu ní úǹ kūū-dì ki ŋɔ́fì malaa malaa.
translated: Abraham took the wood for the burnt offering, and placed it on his son Isaac, and he himself carried the fire and the knife. As the two of them went on together,

ref: Abraham and Sarah were very old, and Sarah was well past the age for having children.
text: Abraam nì u-nimpuu nìn púĺ tikpil, u-nimpuu nìn jítì mmaal pu.
translated: Abraham had two sons, one by the slave woman and one by the free woman.

ref: And Abraham asked him, “Lord, when you destroy the evil people, are you also going to destroy those who are good?
text: Ní Abraam sútì ki pìl̄ ti-Dindaan ní ki bí-u yii: «Mimmee, a làá kúntí binikpitib kí kpáaǹ deedeedambaaa?
translated: Abraham answered and said to Dindaan and his people: "My friends, why are you arguing with me about this matter?"

ref: After speaking with Abraham, the Lord left, and Abraham went back home.
text: Ti-Dindaan sòon̄ nì Abraam míǹ ki dóòyee ní ki búntì, ní Abraam mun nín fàtī u-dumpu.
translated: The angels said to Abraham, "Where is your wife?" and Abraham replied, "She is in the tent."

ref: and the Lord sent burning sulfur down like rain on Sodom and Gomorrah.
text: Niinee ní ti-Dindaan yāntì farwata nyánní yilpu ki fōō ŋŋmi ki sìtī kii utaayee Soodoom nì Goomoora timmu pu.
translated: That is how Dindaan’s daughters made their father drink wine and slept with him without him knowing when they lay down or when they got up; this is how Sodom and Gomorrah were destroyed.

ref: He destroyed those cities and everyone who lived in them, as well as their land and the trees and grass that grew there.
text: U nìn nāaǹ ntimu gbanti nì ditantakal din poon mu bée kɔkɔ, ki kūū ki céetì mu-nib nì isufi nì timool kɔkɔ.
translated: If you want your heart to be at peace and your life to be good, you must forgive and love those who have wronged you.

ref: The next day the older daughter said to her sister, “I slept with my father last night. We'll get him drunk again tonight, so you can sleep with him, and we can each have a child.”
text: Kutaa wúntèe ní ukpil tùkū u-naal yii: «M fu dōoǹ ti-baa cee kunyeeu. Tí gíti nyùntī-u ndaam kunyeeu nee si mun ń cùú dòon̄ u-cee, tí ŋmā kí tīī ti-baa inibool.»
translated: The child said to his father like this: "I want to go with my friends. If you do not allow me to go with them today, I will not speak to you again."

ref: What were you thinking when you did this?
text: Ní ki gíti bí yii: «Ba di yāntì ní a ŋá míǹ?»
translated: He asked him: "Where are you going?"

ref: After Abimelech had given Abraham some sheep, cattle, and slaves, he sent Sarah back
text: Niinee ní Abimeeleeki cúú inaa nì ipii nì iŋoobu nì iyumbu bininjab nì binimpoobi ki tīī-u, ní ki gíiǹ u-nimpuu Saara mun ki tīī-u.
translated: Then Abimelech called Abraham and said to him, "What have you done to us? How have I wronged you that you have brought such great guilt upon me and my kingdom? You have done things to me that should never be done."

ref: Although Abraham was very old, Sarah had a son exactly at the time God had said.
text: Nì min kɔkɔ Abraam nìn púĺ tikpilee, Saara cáá dipool ki màl̄-u kibininjabiki ŋyunti ŋun Unimbɔti nìn pée yóoǹ Abraamee deedee.
translated: When God called Abraham and promised him, Sarah did not believe that she would give birth to the child God had promised Abraham.

ref: Then she sat down a long way off, because she could not bear to watch him die. And she cried bitterly.
text: Ní ki sútì ki cūtìi kāl̀ ŋkpaanceeti, kun puee u lī u-neen yii: «Maa là kí ká kibiki nee nín làá kpú puee.» An puee u kāń ndan ní ki sūǹ ki yīkintí.
translated: When they finished eating the food, the father said to his children: "Do not do what I have just done to your mother." And the children did as their father instructed.

ref: God blessed Ishmael, and as the boy grew older, he became an expert at hunting with his bow and arrows. He lived in the Paran Desert, and his mother chose an Egyptian woman for him to marry. A Peace Treaty
text: U nìn kóò kupɔɔu kun nìn bí Paaran tiŋkinee ní u-na dāa cáá unimpu Eejipiti ki tīī-u. Abraam nì Abimeeleeki pūtì ki pōoǹ tɔb
translated: They settled there because they did not want to stay in Paran, since they had come out of Egypt together. Abraham and Abimelech made an agreement at that place.

ref: And so, Abraham promised he would.
text: Ní Abraam kíí-u yii: «M pūtì yii m làá ŋá míǹ».
translated: But Abraham said to him: "I cannot do that for you."

ref: Abraham gave Abimelech some sheep and cattle, then the two men made a peace treaty.
text: Niinee Abraam cúú ipii nì iŋoobu nì inaa ki tīī Abimeeleeki, ní bi-liitil pūtì ki pōoǹ tɔb.
translated: Then Abraham got up and left with his wife and all he had, and went to Abimelech, who was king of Gerar.

ref: Abraham planted a tamarisk tree in Beersheba and worshiped the eternal Lord God.
text: Ní Abraam cóoǹ busubu bun bi yíì yii tamariisee Beerseeba ní ki jáam̀ ti-Dindaan Unimbɔti un pée bí kí nīn cá jaanjaanee.
translated: Abraham planted a tamarisk tree in Beersheba and there he called on the name of the Lord, the Eternal God.

ref: Isaac said, “Father, we have the coals and the wood, but where is the lamb for the sacrifice?” “My son,” Abraham answered, “God will provide the lamb.” The two of them walked on, and
text: Ní u-baa kíí-u yii: «M-biki, Unimbɔti u-ba di làá tin tīī-ti dipibil din ti làá dūú ŋá saraawɔkaayee.» Ní bi nín nìkì bi-saŋŋu.
translated: He said to her: "My child, God will give you a husband who will take care of you." And she was comforted.

ref: when they reached the place that God had told him about, Abraham built an altar and placed the wood on it. Next, he tied up his son and put him on the wood.
text: Bi bàn̄ laakin Unimbɔti dàkā-bi bí cùee, ní Abraam máń niin nsaraafan, ki kɔ́fì idɔ ki páaǹ ní ki bóob́ u-jipɔɔn Isaaki ki páaǹ idɔee pu.
translated: But when Unimbɔti had finished speaking, Abraham took the wood for the burnt offering, and placed it on his son Isaac, and he himself carried the fire and the knife.

ref: He then took the knife and got ready to kill his son.
text: Abraam ŋá míǹ ki dóòyee ní ki tāntì u-ŋal ki yōoǹ kijiki kí bàà làntì u-jipɔɔn.
translated: Abraham took the wood for the burnt offering and placed it on his son Isaac.

ref: But the Lord's angel shouted from heaven, “Abraham! Abraham!” “Here I am!” he answered.
text: Kpalaayee ní ti-Dindaan tuuŋŋu téenní yilpu ní ki bí yii: «Abraam! Abraam!» Ní u kíí yii: «M sèé».
translated: Then the angel of the Lord called to him from heaven and said, "Abraham! Abraham!" And he answered, "Here I am."

ref: The Lord's angel called out from heaven a second time:
text: Ní ti-Dindaan tuuŋŋu tí yíinní Abraam yilpu nliliitiim
translated: The descendants of Dindaan are more numerous than the children of Abraham.

ref: “Sir, the whole field, including the cave, is yours. With my own people as witnesses, I freely give it to you as a burial place for your dead.”
text: «Aayee, ukpil! Pīlifiǹ-mi, maa làá nyāfiǹ-si kusaau nì ntaŋkpalɔkɔ ŋun bí ku-poonee. M làá dūú pɔ̄-si ní m-nabiyaamu kɔkɔ nimbiin, á cù kí síb́ a-nimpuu.»
translated: Hey, friend! Please, help me carry this basket to the house over there. I want to finish quickly so I can go to the market before it gets dark.

ref: So Abraham buried his wife Sarah in Machpelah Cave that was in the field
text: Míǹ boonee ní Abraam cáá u-nimpuu Saara ki síb́ ntaŋkpalɔkɔ gbanti poon, Makipeela saakun Mamiree caŋin, an dàkà yii Eebrɔn, Kanaan yab tiŋkin.
translated: They buried Abraham and his wife Sarah in the cave of Machpelah, near Mamre, which is at Hebron, in the land of Canaan.

ref: One day, Abraham called in his most trusted servant and said to him, “Solemnly promise me
text: Dalbee, Abraam yíiń u-tɔntɔnliu un yúntì u-cee ki sá ukpil ki kpèé u-wanti kɔkɔee ní ki bí-u yii: «Dū a-ŋal kí tíkiń m-tapandi taapu.
translated: The next day, Abraham got up early in the morning, saddled his donkey, and took two of his young men with him, along with his son Isaac. He split wood for the burnt offering and set out for the place God had told him about.

ref: She answered, “My father is Bethuel, the son of Nahor and Milcah.
text: Ní usapɔɔnee kíí-u yii: «M-baa di sá Beetuweel, Naakɔɔr nì u-nimpuu Miilka jipɔɔn.»
translated: She also said to him: “We have both straw and fodder, and room to spend the night.”

ref: “I thank you, Lord God of my master Abraham! You have led me to his relatives and kept your promise to him.”
text: ní ki bí yii: «M nyɔ́ŋkí ti-Dindaan, m-kpiliu Abraam Nimbɔtiu un ŋáań-u tiŋan múlmul kaa gbèentée! Úǹ di tí kpōotì-mi ki cáań ki kɔ́ǹ m-kpiliu nabiyaamu cindin táb.»
translated: And he said: "I beg you, father Abraham, send Lazarus to my family’s house! For I have five brothers, so that he may warn them, lest they also come into this place of torment."

ref: The servant explained: I am Abraham's servant.
text: Ní utɔntɔnlee bí yii: «Man sá Abraam tɔntɔnliu ní.
translated: The rich man said: "Father Abraham, have mercy on me."

ref: But if they refuse to let her come back with you, then you are freed from your promise.”
text: U gíti bí-mi yii: <A yaa cūtì m-dumpu, m-cindi poon ní baa kíí kí tīiń-si usapɔɔnee, a nyáń dipùtil din m yāntì a pūtèe bɔtin.>
translated: He/she told me this: <If you want to eat yam, you must first peel it, wash it well and cook it before you can eat it.>

ref: “Let Rebekah stay with us for a week or ten days,” they answered. “Then she may go.”
text: Ní usapɔɔn niija nì u-na bí-u yii: «Yàntī usapɔɔn ń gíti kàl̄ ti-cee iwiŋkaaŋkaa kii iwiin saalaa pu. Míǹ boonee ní á nín kpáaǹ-u kí búntí.»
translated: If someone tells you: "Take this thing and go and put it in the room over there and come back quickly. I don't want anyone else to see you do it."

ref: They gave Rebekah their blessing and said, “We pray that God will give you many children and grandchildren and that he will help them defeat their enemies.”
text: Bi làá yīkèe ní Reebeeka yab mēē ki bīiǹ u-pu yii: «Sii ti-niisal, á kpántí nnibiim na. A-naantiibi ń dāá nīn pɔ̄ɔǹ bi-nannanliibi!»
translated: When her days to give birth were completed, behold, there were twins in her womb. The first one came out red, all his body like a hairy cloak; so they called his name Esau.

ref: Isaac took Rebekah into the tent where his mother had lived before she died, and Rebekah became his wife. He loved her and was comforted over the loss of his mother.
text: Ní Isaaki yōoǹ Reebeeka ki cáa kɔ́ǹ u-na Saara tantiun, ní ki dū-u ki kpántì u-nimpuu ní ki néeń-u. An di ŋméeǹ Isaaki u-na kúm.
translated: When Isaac and Rebekah were old and Sarah had died, they buried her in the cave and mourned for her. That is where Isaac's mother was buried.

ref: and they had six sons: Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
text: U nìn màl̄-u Siimran nì Yɔkisan nì Meedan nì Madiyan nì Yiisbaaki nì Suuwa.
translated: And these are the sons of Simran: Yokisan, Meedan, Madiyan, Yisbak, and Suwa.

ref: While Abraham was still alive, he gave gifts to the sons of Hagar and Keturah. He also sent their sons to live in the east far from his son Isaac, and when Abraham died, he left everything to Isaac. The Death of Abraham
text: ní ki pú u-nimpoobi bin u nìn gíti cáá boonee biyaamu mun tiwan tiba. Ŋyunti ŋun Abraam bí kaa láá kpíìyee ní u yāntì u-nimpoobee biyaamu yíkì ki búntì ndan, ŋwìiń nyalaŋki pú, kí nīn dátiǹ u-jipɔɔn Isaaki. Abraam kúm
translated: And he took the firewood for the burnt offering and placed it on his son Isaac, and he himself carried the fire and the knife. As the two of them went on together, Isaac spoke up and said to his father Abraham, "Father?" "Yes, my son?" Abraham replied.

ref: Isaac was the son of Abraham,
text: Abraam jipɔɔn Isaaki biyaamu mun u màl̄ee bɔti sèé. Abraam màl̄ Isaaki.
translated: Abraham called his son Isaac and told him something. Abraham loved Isaac.

ref: and they stopped up the wells that Abraham's servants had dug before his death.
text: Ní Fiiliistii yab dū ntam ki gbíŋkì lɔkɔb bin kɔkɔ u-baa Abraam nìn yāntì u-tɔntɔnliibi gbìn̄ ŋyunti ŋun u dáá bée.
translated: The Philistines stopped up all the wells that Abraham’s servants had dug, filling them with earth.

ref: Isaac's servants dug another well, and the shepherds also quarreled about it. So that well was named “Jealous.”
text: Ní u-tɔntɔnliibi gíti gbìn̄ lɔkɔ ubɔ ní bi tí dɔ́miń ki táká-bi, ní Isaaki pú lɔkɔ gbanti mun diyindi yii Siitina, taapu di sá yii <Bi jáń nì mi.>
translated: He moved from there and dug another well, but they did not quarrel over it. So he named it Rehoboth, saying, "Now the Lord has given us room."

ref: When they arrived, Isaac asked, “Why are you here? Didn't you send me away because you hated me?”
text: Ní Isaaki bí-bi yii: «Naa là m-bɔti ní ki jìn̄-mi ni-tiŋkin, ni gíti nyàab̀ ba m-cee doo?»
translated: Then Isaac said: "Why have you come to me, since you hate me and sent me away from you?"

ref: Early the next morning Isaac and the others made a solemn agreement, then he let them go in peace.
text: Ku wúntì kutaafaauee ní bi pūtì ki pōoǹ tɔb. Míǹ boonee ní Isaaki nyántì-bi nsanni, ní bi nín kùǹ nì dipɔɔsɔŋkil.
translated: If you want to succeed in life, you must work hard. That is what Isaac's mother always told him, and he never forgot her advice.

ref: and gave him some bread and the tasty food she had cooked.
text: Ní ki dū tijin tin u ŋée nì kpɔnɔb bin u púĺee ki dūu ŋúbiǹ Jaakɔb ú cáá tīī u-baa.
translated: And he gave them food and water because they were tired from the journey Jacob had taken with his family.

ref: Jacob went closer. His father touched him and said, “You sound like Jacob, but your hands feel hairy like Esau's.”
text: Ní u sútì ki pìl̄ u-baa ní u-baa máfì máfì-u, ní ki bí yii: «Nneen sá Jaakɔb neen, ama ní iŋal sá Eesayuu ŋalii.»
translated: He did not recognize him because his hands were hairy like his brother Esau’s hands; so he blessed him.

ref: Then Isaac said, “Son, come over here and kiss me.”
text: U jíń ki dóòyee ní ki bí Jaakɔb yii: «M-jipɔɔn, sútiń kí dāá kpàl̄-mi.»
translated: He answered and said to Jacob: "My friend, listen to my words."

ref: God will bless you, my son, with dew from heaven and with fertile fields, rich with grain and grapes.
text: <Unimbɔti ń tīī a-kpaatanti nsɔŋfi ŋun nyēeń yilpuee, kí yàntī a-tiŋki ń nīn gàm̀ kí lòoǹ tiŋan, tikpaajin nì ndamam ń gbóotí a-cee.
translated: If you want to achieve something important in life, you must work hard and not let difficulties discourage you.

ref: Esau replied, “My brother deserves the name Jacob, because he has already cheated me twice. The first time he cheated me out of my rights as the first-born son, and now he has cheated me out of my blessing.” Then Esau asked his father, “Don't you have any blessing left for me?”
text: Ní Eesayuu bí yii: «Bi nín yíì-u yii Jaakɔb <Uŋmatinlee> pu ní u líbitì-mi míǹ mili kɔkɔɔɔ? U nìn fōō m-kpiti, ní kɔŋkɔnnee u tí fōō m-biindi. Mimmee, m-baa, aa kíǹ-mi dibiindi dibaaa?»
translated: And Esau said: "Is he not rightly named Jacob <Supplanter> for he has deceived me these two times? He took my birthright, and now he has taken my blessing. Haven’t you reserved any blessing for me?"

ref: Now listen carefully and do what I say. Go to the home of my brother Laban in Haran
text: An puee m-biki, á pīlifì m nín làá sāŋkì-si puee! Yīkìí sāǹ kí nīn cá m-niija Laaban cee, Kaaran tiŋkin.
translated: If you wake up, you should thank God for waking up! You don't know what happened to others at night, some have died.

ref: and stay with him for a while. When Esau stops being angry
text: Á nīn bí niin kí dāá sāā ŋyunti ŋun a-maan ŋuul làá dāá sɔ̀ŋkèe.
translated: If you do not work hard, you will not achieve anything in life.

ref: Go at once to your mother's father Bethuel in northern Syria and choose a wife from one of the daughters of Laban, your mother's brother.
text: Ní ki bí-u yii: «Yīkì kí nīn cá Padan-Aram, a-na baa Beetuweel cee kí kpèè a-wee Laaban sapɔmbi kansikin ubɔ kí cáá.
translated: He gave him this order: "You must not take a wife from the daughters of Padan-Aram, but go to the house of Bethuel, your mother's father, and take a wife from there from the daughters of Laban, your mother's brother."

ref: Esau found out that his father Isaac had blessed Jacob and had warned him not to marry any of the Canaanite women. He also learned that Jacob had been sent to find a wife in northern Syria
text: Eesayuu nìn ká yii Isaaki mēē ki bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon ki bí yii ú nīn cá Padan-Aram kí cáá unimpu niin, ní ki tīī-u mara yii ú taa cáá unimpu Kanaan yab sapɔmbi kansikin.
translated: Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him away to Padan-Aram to take a wife from there, and that as he blessed him he commanded him, saying, "You shall not take a wife from the daughters of Canaan."

ref: In a dream he saw a ladder that reached from earth to heaven, and God's angels were going up and down on it.
text: U dɔ̄ee ní ki dámintì yii akɔl yú kitiŋ ki tin sāā kutagbɔŋu ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi tà ki jɔ́ḿ ki kpákatiń.
translated: You must not do anything that will bring shame to your family or to the community where you live and work.

ref: he looked out in a field and saw a well where shepherds took their sheep for water. Three flocks of sheep were lying around the well, which was covered with a large rock.
text: U kpēēyee, lɔkɔ ubɔ dɔ̄ akpaanni tipicaŋkpun tita dɔ̄ ki kɔ́ĺ. Niin ní bipikpaaliibi nìn lúù nnyim ki nyùntī ipii. Ditaŋkpakpaandi diba nìn pīī lɔkɔee nyɔkɔpu.
translated: In the morning, people usually gather together to discuss important matters. They share their thoughts and listen to each other's opinions. These discussions help them find good solutions to their problems.

ref: But they replied, “We can't do that until they all get here, and the rock has been rolled away from the well.”
text: Ní bi kíí-u yii: «Taa ŋūǹ ki ŋá míǹ, asee ipii kɔkɔ yaa bāań ki kpáfì. Niinee ní tí nín cúú ditaŋkpal kí bíntí ní kí nyùntī-i.»
translated: He did not say to him: "Take this for me, and when you arrive there, give it to the chief. If he asks you who sent you, tell him it was me."

ref: While Jacob was still talking with the men, his cousin Rachel came up with her father's sheep.
text: Bi yú ki sòoǹ míǹ ní Raseel dūu bāań nì u-baa piii, di sá Raseel nìn sá upikpaal ní.
translated: If you see someone in Raseel doing something bad, tell Raseel about it.

ref: Laban said, “You are my nephew, and you are like one of my own family.” Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel After Jacob had been there for a month,
text: Ní Laaban bí Jaakɔb yii: «Tiŋman, a baasii sá m-niliu ní kiŋkiŋ.» Jaakɔb nìn kāl̀ Laaban cee ki sāā uŋmal ubɔ. Jaakɔb cáá Leeya nì Raseel
translated: Then Laban said to Jacob, "Truly, you are my own flesh and blood." Jacob stayed with Laban for a month. Jacob loved Leah and Rachel.

ref: Laban said to him, “You shouldn't have to work without pay, just because you are a relative of mine. What do you want me to give you?”
text: Dalbee ní Laaban bí Jaakɔb yii: «A nín sá m-niliuee puee, á nīn tūǹ ditundi míǹ faalaa ki tìì-maaa? An puee tùkù-mi kun a là ḿ dāá pà-see.»
translated: Then Laban said to Jacob: "Because you are my relative, should you serve me for nothing? Tell me, what should your wages be?"

ref: So Laban gave a big feast and invited all their neighbors.
text: Ní Laaban kóoǹ niin yab kɔkɔ ki kpáfì ní bi jíń tinimpucan jiŋaaliu.
translated: Laaban said that he would go to the farm if it did not rain in the morning.

ref: At the end of the week of celebration, Laban let Jacob marry Rachel, and he gave her his servant woman Bilhah. Jacob loved Rachel more than he did Leah, but he had to work another seven years for Laban.
text: Ní Jaakɔb nì Raseel mun kpáfì. Jaakɔb nìn néeń Raseel ki jítiǹ Leeya, ní ki nìn gíti kāl̀ abin aluli ki túń ditundi ki tīī Laaban. Jaakɔb biyaamu bɔti
translated: Jacob loved Rachel. Jacob wanted Rachel more than Leah, so he worked for seven years for Laban in order to marry Rachel. Jacob was patient.

ref: Leah gave birth to a son and named him Reuben. Then she said, “The Lord has taken away my sorrow. Now my husband will love me more than he does Rachel.”
text: Leeya nìn dūu cáá dipool ki màl̄ kibininjabiki, ní ki pú-kì diyindi yii Rubeen. Di sá yii un nìn lī yii: «Ti-Dindaan ká m-falaau, kɔŋkɔnnee m-cal làá nīn kíǹ ki néeń-mi.»
translated: When she gave birth, she called his name Rubeen, for she said, “The Lord has seen my misery; surely my husband will love me now.”

ref: and they had a son.
text: Ní Biila cáá dipool ní ki màl̄ Jaakɔb kibininjabiki.
translated: God blessed Jacob abundantly.

ref: That evening when Jacob came in from the fields, Leah told him, “You're sleeping with me tonight. I hired you with my son's love flowers.” They slept together that night,
text: Kujoou Jaakɔb nyánní kusaau ki kùnnée ní Leeya túŋkì-u nsanni ní ki bí-u yii: «Man cee ní a làá dòon̄ díǹ. Kí ŋmā kí ká-si á dòon̄ m-ceeyee, m-jipɔɔn mandragɔɔr biliŋ ní m dūu pàn̄ m-yɔnti.» Ní Jaakɔb lafun dōoǹ u-cee kunyeeu gbanti.
translated: During the wheat harvest, Reuben went out and found some mandrake plants in the field and brought them to his mother Leah. Rachel said to Leah, "Please give me some of your son's mandrakes." But Jacob heard their conversation and remained silent.

ref: Jacob answered: You've seen how hard I've worked for you, and you know how your flocks and herds have grown under my care.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «Sii a-ba nyí m nín túń ditundi din ki tīī-see nì m nín ŋúb́ a-waŋkuti tì būtì miŋyee.
translated: And Jacob said to them: "If you do not help me in this matter, I will not be able to accomplish what I have planned."

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Di sá yii kí wàatī ḿ bàńee, tin a nìn cááyee kaa nìn náaǹ báà fiii. Ama kí yòon̄ ŋwiin ŋun m-taal tāā a-dumpuee, a-wanti wíiǹ tikpil ní ti-Dindaan bīiǹ a-boon. Too, kɔŋkɔnnee ŋyunti ŋulɔŋu ní man làá gíti dūú tɔ̄ ditundi kí ká tin làá ŋmàntī ḿ kpèè m-dicaŋkuee?»
translated: If this is really what happened, then you should not be worried or afraid. But if someone else comes and says something different, you must listen carefully and compare what they say with what Dindaan’s child said. Also, sometimes people say things that are not true just to make others afraid or to deceive them, so how can you know what is really true?

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bátì isufi in bi yíì peepiliiyee nì amandi nì plataanee pambiyaamu nsɔmu, ki cùkū cùkū-mu ki māntìi tú ki dāa kátì taapu, ní ki pɔ́kɔtì ajan ŋin u cùkūee, ní ndɔsɔmuee kíǹ ki cáá mpim mpim.
translated: When Jacob woke up early in the morning, he took the stone he had placed under his head, set it up as a pillar, and poured oil on top of it, and he named that place Bethel.

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun ní tiwaŋkuti tin yáàyee yaa làá jɔ̄m̀ tɔbee, ní Jaakɔb ń yòon̄ní ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee kí ŋá tiwan tin poon tiwaŋkuti nyùee kí túĺ ti-nimbiin, ní tì jɔ́ḿ tɔb ndɔsɔmu mun u cùkūee cee.
translated: This is not because they are all Abraham’s children, but, “Through Isaac your offspring will be reckoned.” In other words, it is not the children by physical descent who are God’s children, but it is the children of the promise who are regarded as Abraham’s offspring.

ref: But he would not put out the branches when the weaker animals were mating. So Jacob got all of the healthy animals, and Laban got what was left.
text: Ama tiwaŋkuti tin sá ŋkaŋkalimuee yaa bāańee, waa yōontí ndɔsɔmuee ti-nimbiin tí jɔ̄m̀ tɔb. Ŋkaŋkalimu gbantee, Laaban yati dee, ní úǹ yati sá tin yáàyee. Jaakɔb sáǹ Laaban
translated: Ama said that she would not go with them, but she would wait until they returned. When they left, Laban asked her if she would go with them. Jacob and Laban

ref: Jacob soon became rich and successful. He owned many sheep, goats, camels, and donkeys, as well as a lot of slaves.
text: Míǹ ní Jaakɔb ŋá ki kpántì ugaajakpaŋkpaan ki cáá tiwaŋkuti tikpil, ki cáá bitɔntɔnliibi binimpoobi nì bininjab nì igutaamu nì mmamu.
translated: We are Jacob who does not follow the ways of the world, who does not worship idols or images or spirits or ancestors.

ref: Jacob also noticed that Laban was not as friendly as he had been before.
text: Jaakɔb nín kpēē Laaban nimbiinee, u dáań ki ká yii u nì u kansikin kaa ti mɔ̄ kii dooooyee an nín nìn bí puee.
translated: Jacob deceived Laban by not telling him that he was running away, so he left with all he had.

ref: and that he keeps cheating me by changing my wages time after time. But God has protected me.
text: Ní an yaa bàà sá ú pà-mi puee ní ú cúú kpántí. U kpántì-mi míǹ ní áli ŋfunyɔm saalaa pu, ama Unimbɔti kaa kíí ú ŋá-mi digaŋgam.
translated: If someone does not have patience, they will make mistakes. Mistakes can lead to many problems, but wisdom helps to avoid them.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: Ŋyunti ŋun tiwaŋkuti bíi jɔ́ḿ tɔbee ní m ká kudamintiiu poon yii ijaa in bíi jɔ́ḿ isalee sá atan atan nì iluntoon iluntoon nì ŋmàtì ŋmati.
translated: The person who is wise is not the one who knows many things but the one who understands and applies what he knows little by little and step by step.

ref: Then Jacob, his wives, and his children got on camels and left
text: Niinee ní Jaakɔb yōoǹ u-biyaamu nì u-nimpoobi ki kāaǹ igutaamu pu kí yīkì.
translated: This is how Jacob got his children and his descendants after him.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Niinee ní Laaban yōoǹ u-nabiyaamu ní bi nín jì Jaakɔb boon. Bi cōom̀ iwiin iluli ní ki tin nín cúú-u Galaadi jool pu.
translated: That night, Laban came to Jacob’s tent. Jacob had set up his tent on the mountain, and Laban and his relatives camped in the hill country of Gilead.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Ama un cee ní a yaa ká a-waaŋee, bí kpɔ̀-u. Ti-nabiyaamu kɔkɔ di bí nee. Dāan̄ m-cee kí kpèè a yaa ká a-wanti tiba á yòon̄.» Jaakɔb kaa nìn nyí yii Raseel di yùn̄ awaa.
translated: Ama said to her, "Is it not enough that you have taken my husband? Will you also take my son's mandrakes?" Rachel said, "He may sleep with you tonight in exchange for your son's mandrakes." Jacob came in from the fields that evening, and Leah went out to meet him.

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Tiba di yaa cúú a-waŋkuluuku kubɔee, maa cāabìń-si diwaŋkpil, see ḿ nyàab̀ kí téeǹ kun an cúúyee yil. A-waŋkuti tin bi yù ŋwiimpuee nì tin bi yù kunyeeuee, a bàlifī-mi ní ḿ téeǹ-si.
translated: When they arrived at the chief's house, they greeted him, and he asked what had brought them to his house. The chief said that if they had come for peace or for trouble, he would listen to what they had to say.

ref: So I am ready to make an agreement with you, and we will pile up some large rocks here to remind us of the agreement.”
text: An ŋmāntì man nì si tí sòoǹ kí bóob́ kí pòon̄ tɔb, án nīn sá kudaaŋŋu man nì si kansikin.»
translated: If someone does not know where he is going, he will end up where he did not intend to go, and he will not know how to explain where he is.

ref: he told his men to get some more rocks and pile them up next to it. Then Jacob and Laban ate a meal together beside the rocks.
text: Ní ki bí u-nabiyaamu yii bí tòò ataŋkpee kí bíĺ dikpúkul. Bi tōō ki bíl̀ee ní bi-kɔkɔ kāl̀ di-pu ki jíń tijin.
translated: He did not go to the farm because he was sick. That is why he stayed at home and did not join them.

ref: Laban named the pile of rocks Jegar Sahadutha. But Jacob named it Galeed.
text: Ní Laaban pú ditaŋkpakpukulee diyindi u-sooŋyi poon yii Yeegar-Saaduta, ní Jaakɔb mun yíiń-dì u-sooŋyi poon yii Galeedi.
translated: When Laban called the heap of stones Jegar-Sahadutha, Jacob called the heap of stones Galeed.

ref: Laban also said, “This pile of rocks means that the Lord will watch us both while we are apart from each other.” So the place was also named Mizpah.
text: Bi gíti pú niin diyindi yii Miisipa, taapu di sá yii: <Unimbɔti ń nīn bí ki wāĺ-ti.> Di sá Laaban nìn bí yii: «Unimbɔti ń nīn bí ki wāĺ man nì si kansikin ti yaa kaa dāa gíti bí ki wāĺ tɔbee.
translated: They all sat there at Mizpah, and he said this: "God is witness between you and me." Then Laban said to him: "God is witness between you and me today, so that when we are apart from each other, God is witness between you and me."

ref: My father Nahor, your grandfather Abraham, and their ancestors all worshiped the same God, and he will make sure that we each keep the agreement. Then Jacob made a promise in the name of the fearsome God his father Isaac had worshiped.
text: Abraam nì Naakɔɔr Nimbɔtiu un sá bi-baa Nimbɔtiuee ń dāá sòoǹ man nì sii kansikin bɔti.» Ní Jaakɔb pūtì Unimbɔti un u-baa Isaaki nìn jáaḿ ki fàŋkée.
translated: Abraham and Nahor both had descendants, and their descendants were blessed. And Jacob became a descendant and was blessed through Isaac as well.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: M pátì kí fōō tiŋan tin a ŋá-mi nee nì tibulcin tin a dàkā man a-tɔntɔnliu nee, kun puee ŋkpandɔ baba ní m nìn ŋúb́ ki dūtì Jɔɔdan buŋŋu nee. Ama díǹ pu nee, m yākatì m-nib nì m-waŋkuti akiti ali ní nee.
translated: When I woke up this morning, I remembered the promise I made to my friend yesterday, so I quickly prepared myself to go to Jordan's house. But before that, I made sure to eat and drink some water first.

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Ní Jaakɔb bí-u yii: «M gbāam̀-si, tùkù-mi a-yindi.» Ní u bí-u yii: «Ba pu ní a là kí béé m-yindi?» Niin niin ní u bīiǹ Jaakɔb boon.
translated: And Jacob said to him: "Feed me, I am hungry." And he said to him: "Why should I feed you if you are not hungry?" That is how he answered Jacob.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,
translated: And he answered them saying: "I heard Esau saying to himself: <Why should I lose my life because of her? What good will it do me to kill my brother?>,

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ní Jaakɔb mun nín nìkì u-saŋŋu. U cée ní Unimbɔti tuuŋyi cílìń u-pu nsanni.
translated: But Jacob did not know it. He thought Unimboti was sleeping in his room.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Niinee ní Jaakɔb lēē binib ki tú u-maan Eesayuu cee Eedoom satin, Seeyiir tiŋki poon.

translated: And he asked them again: "Do you know a man named Laban, the son of Nahor?" They replied, "Yes, we know him and he is well."

ref: Jacob was so frightened that he divided his people, sheep, cattle, and camels into two groups.

translated: They called him by the name Esau because he was hairy all over; then they said: "This is how we will call him Esau. He was born with his body covered with hair."

ref: He thought, “If Esau attacks one group, perhaps the other can escape.”
text: Niinee ní tifaandi kɔ́ Jaakɔb ní u-pɔbil bíil̀. Ní u cúú u-nib nì u-piii nì u-ŋoobui nì u-naai nì u-gutaamui ki yākatì akiti ali.
translated: This is the story of Jacob and his twelve sons. He had Reuben and Simeon and Levi and Judah and Issachar and Zebulun with Leah his wife.

ref: Then he said to the servant in charge of the first herd, “When Esau meets you, he will ask whose servant you are. He will want to know where you are going and who owns those animals in front of you.

translated: He told them this story so that they would always pray and not give up: “There was a judge in a certain town who neither feared God nor cared about people.”

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Ní u kpáafì un līīyee yii: «M-maan Eesayuu yaa tú-si nsanni ní ki yaa bālifì-si yii: <A-kpiliu sá ŋma ní a cá la? Ŋma di yì tiwaŋkuti tin līī a-nyɔkɔpu nee?>,
translated: This is how it happened: "When Esau heard his father's words, he cried out loudly and bitterly: <My father, bless me too! Bless me as well!>"

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Ní unaaciinee bālifì-u yii: «Bi yíì-si yii ba ní?» Ní u kíí-u yii: «Bi yíì-mi yii Jaakɔb ní.»
translated: He asked him, “Whose son are you?” He replied, “I am Jacob’s son.”

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Kutaa wúntì an dáá bɔ̀ǹee ní Laaban yíkì ki kpàl̄ u-bisalib nì u-naantiibi ki kāaǹ-bi, ki mēē ki bīiǹ bi-pu ní ki fātìi nín kùǹ u-dumpu. Jaakɔb bɔ́ntì kí tūŋkì Eesayuu
translated: Jacob stayed with Laban and worked for him tending his sheep and goats, and he married his daughters and had children with them and became very wealthy. Jacob was afraid of Esau.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
